2026-06-07T10:52:07.0518626Z [36;1mset -euo pipefail[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0519047Z [36;1m# actions/download-artifact (>=v5) flattens contents directly into `path/`[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0519595Z [36;1m# whenever exactly one artifact matches the pattern, ignoring[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0520104Z [36;1m# `merge-multiple: false`. Re-shape into the per-group subdir layout so[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0520922Z [36;1m# downstream validation sees a stable structure regardless of matrix size.[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0521347Z [36;1m# Upstream reference:[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0521799Z [36;1m# https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/blob/v8.0.1/src/download-artifact.ts[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0522407Z [36;1m# (see the `isSingleArtifactDownload || mergeMultiple || artifacts.length === 1`[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0522967Z [36;1m# branch). Remove this step once download-artifact stops flattening or[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0523409Z [36;1m# exposes an opt-out.[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0523777Z [36;1mexpected_count=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq '.group // [] | length')[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0524186Z [36;1mif [ "$expected_count" -eq 1 ]; then[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0524602Z [36;1m  group_id=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq -r '.group[0].id')[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0525079Z [36;1m  # Defence-in-depth: group_id is interpolated into a shell path. apm-prep[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0525585Z [36;1m  # produces a sanitised id today, but enforce a strict allowlist here so[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0526064Z [36;1m  # any future schema drift cannot smuggle traversal sequences.[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0526514Z [36;1m  if ! printf '%s' "$group_id" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$'; then[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0526975Z [36;1m    echo "::error::unsafe group_id '$group_id' (must match ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$)"[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0527359Z [36;1m    exit 1[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0527582Z [36;1m  fi[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0527914Z [36;1m  group_dir="/tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles/${ARTIFACT_PREFIX}apm-${group_id}"[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0528305Z [36;1m  if [ ! -d "$group_dir" ]; then[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0528785Z [36;1m    mkdir -p "$group_dir"[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0529277Z [36;1m    find /tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -path "$group_dir" -exec mv {} "$group_dir/" \;[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:07.0529784Z [36;1m  fi[0m

2026-06-07T10:52:19.5930919Z [36;1m# Generate a secure random API key (360 bits of entropy, 40+ chars)[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:19.5931397Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:19.5931832Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:19.5932193Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:19.5932479Z [36;1m[0m
2026-06-07T10:52:19.5932689Z [36;1mPORT=3001[0m

2026-06-07T11:00:52.5362384Z       "rationale": "canvas_integrator._emit_trust_block calls diagnostics.warn() (CATEGORY_WARNING), which renders yellow [!] AFTER collision/overwrite warnings in _CATEGORY_ORDER (security > policy > auth > drift > collision > overwrite > warning). Blocking executable Node code from a dependency is semantically identical to an MCP trust block -- it is a policy-level security gate, not a generic warning. Using CATEGORY_POLICY with severity='block' would render red [x] N dependency blocked by org policy, appear earlier in the summary, and match how other executable-code gates are surfaced. Under the current code a user who skims the yellow section may miss that deployable extension code was silently withheld.",

2026-06-07T11:03:26.7160365Z       "rationale": "shutil.copyfile(src, dest) follows dest if it is a symlink, writing to the symlink target. The pre-copy non_file guard (line 317-328) skips bundles where dest.exists() and not dest.is_file(); however dest.is_file() returns True for a symlink-to-regular-file, so a symlink placed at a planned destination between the planning pass (_plan_bundle_files) and the copy loop could redirect a canvas file write to an arbitrary location outside canvas_root. The ensure_path_within check runs at planning time against the unresolved dest, not at copy time. Canvas alone is created inline at services.py:580 (canvas=CanvasIntegrator()), so the call site in phases/integrate.py:335 cannot reuse ctx.integrators['canvas'], and test authors cannot substitute a mock through the public function interface without patching the class directly. This violates the Inject collaborators via constructor / prefer composition principle in the codebase (same DI seam already working for the other six).\n\nDesign patterns used in this PR:\n- Used: Base+subclass -- CanvasIntegrator(BaseIntegrator); established pattern, correctly applied; the four-gate Chain of Responsibility inside integrate_canvases_for_target (flag -> target -> scope -> trust)● Read (Test-coverage-expert agent — Test coverage review of canvas integrator)

2026-06-07T11:10:55.1571412Z   "arbitration": "All eight panelists converge cleanly: no blocking findings, no correctness regression, no architectural fault that compounds. The canvas trust model -- feature flag as the coarse gate, `--trust-canvas-extensions` as the explicit operator opt-in for dependency canvases, first-party deploy freely once the flag is on -- is principled, no-spoof-proof, and correctly threaded through `InstallContext` (supply-chain, auth-expert). Python-architect confirms the `BaseIntegrator` subclass pattern is followed; the DI inconsistency in `integrate_local_content` is a testability concern only, not a runtime defect. The overall shape of this PR is solid enough to land after the draft phase with targeted pre-exit fixes.\n\nTwo gaps stand above the rest on risk grounds. First, `security.md` now carries an actively false claim: 'APM does not execute scripts.' Canvas is exactly that -- executable Node.js deployed by APM. Doc-writer identified a double failure: the cross-link from `canvas.md` delivers no canvas content, and the statement it implicitly endorses is incorrect for the new surface. An enterprise auditor landing on that cross-link gets a false assurance picture. This is a documentation trust defect that must be corrected before the draft exits -- removing the dangling cross-link and adding a canvas exception callout to `security.md` under 'What APM does NOT do.' Second, the integration tier of the trust gate has zero test coverage. Supply-chain and test-coverage converge on this: for a surface that deploys executable code, APM's tier-floor matrix requires `integration-with-fixtures`. All existing coverage is unit-only (monkeypatched config, `tmp_path`). A future refactor mis-threading `trust_canvas` or `is_first_party` would not be caught until runtime. The drift-exclusion logic in `drift.py:589-646` compounds this -- it has no regression trap at any tier, meaning a refactor could silently re-surface deployed canvas paths as orphaned findings in `apm audit --check drift` with no automated signal. Both carry `evidence.outcome: missing` on `secure-by-default` / `governed-by-policy` surfaces and rank above any opinion-tier recommended finding.\n\nThe remaining recommended gaps are real but lower urgency: three CLI reference docs are missing the `--trust-canvas-extensions` entry (install.md, experimental.md, unpack.md -- all static-tier `evidence.outcome: missing`), `apm install` silently accepts the flag when the canvas feature gate is off while `apm unpack` correctly emits a two-case diagnostic, the trust-block diagnostic fires at `CATEGORY_WARNING` instead of `CATEGORY_POLICY` (wrong visual weight for an executable-code security gate), the `/clear` session-reload hint in both `canvas.md` and `services.py` likely misleads users since `/clear` does not restart the CLI process or re-trigger extension discovery, and the CHANGELOG is missing `by @sergio-sisternes-epam` per project convention. A future refactor mis-threading trust_canvas or is_first_party would not be caught until runtime. Add a tests/integration/ fixture-backed test that runs apm install without and with --trust-canvas-extensions against a real local dep shipping a canvas. Evidence.outcome: missing on secure-by-default and governed-by-policy surfaces."

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7494573Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7495447Z   One critical concern I see: the `_emit_trust_block` in `canvas_integrator.py` calls `diagnostics.warn()`, which puts it in `CATEGORY_WARNING`. This means the warning is rendered by `_render_warning_group` which shows the full message inline for each item. 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7496367Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7497665Z   The full message includes the package name, blocked count, extension names, deploy directory, and the flag to use, so that's working as intended. Checking how the flag definitions differ - `--trust-canvas-extensions` includes the `[experimental]` prefix while `--trust-transitive-mcp` doesn't, which suggests inconsistent labeling of experimental features. 2026-06-07T11:20:27.7624700Z [325] ✓ $ grep -n "_make_project\|monkeypatch\|patch\|MagicMock\|Mock" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/tests/unit/install/test_install_local_bundle.py | head -40
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7625234Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7625620Z [326] ◐ Now I have enough context to write a thorough CLI logging expert review. Let me analyze the key findings:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7626262Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7626606Z   **1. Trust-block warning uses `CATEGORY_WARNING` instead of `CATEGORY_POLICY`**
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7627006Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7627224Z   In `canvas_integrator.py` line 416:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7627505Z   ```python
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7627722Z   diagnostics.warn(
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7627956Z       message=(...),
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7628189Z       package=pkg,
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7628412Z   )
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7628606Z   ```
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7628798Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7630726Z   This calls `DiagnosticCollector.warn()` which puts the item in `CATEGORY_WARNING`. The `_render_warning_group()` method renders these by printing the full message inline without any grouping or count summary. For a single trust-block warning, this works. But there's a subtle issue: the existing `CATEGORY_POLICY` category already exists (see `diagnostics.py`), has a proper renderer `_render_policy_group()` that groups blocked items with a red `[x] N dependency blocked by org policy` header, and appears earlier in `_CATEGORY_ORDER`. 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7632550Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7636807Z   The trust blocks are fundamentally a policy decision—executable code being blocked by default—which gives them semantic weight beyond a generic warning. Using `CATEGORY_WARNING` places them below all other categories in the ordering and obscures their security significance. The warning renderer outputs each item with a `[!]` prefix and the full diagnostic text inline, but this loses the grouped presentation and visual hierarchy that `CATEGORY_POLICY` provides with its dedicated header and count summary. The silent no-op behavior when the canvas flag is disabled works as intended—it doesn't inflate the skip count or emit warnings. But there's a naming inconsistency I'm noticing: the result dictionary uses the key `"canvases"` while the integrator method is called `integrate_canvases_for_target`, which could cause confusion when tracking these values across the codebase. I need to trace through the dispatch and mapping more carefully to understand how the counter key gets set for canvas primitives and where the result dictionary keys are actually defined.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7640387Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7640867Z [327] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/in..., view_range: [280, 350])
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7641207Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7641599Z [328] ✓ grep(-C: 5, -n: true, output_mode: content, paths: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/in..., ...)

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7652687Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7654155Z [335] ◐ Now I need to understand how the `counter_key` works for canvas. Looking at the code, I see that the dispatch dict maps `"canvas"` to `_INTEGRATOR_KWARGS["canvas"]`, and then `result[_entry.counter_key] += _int_result.files_integrated`. The `_entry` is `_dispatch[_prim_name]` which maps to a `PrimitiveMapping`. The `counter_key` on `PrimitiveMapping` determines which key in the result dict is incremented.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7655504Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7655823Z   But looking at the result dict initialization (line 207-213), the keys are:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7656207Z   - "skills"
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7656421Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7656600Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7656793Z   - "sub_skills"
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7657012Z   - "instructions"
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7657236Z   - "commands"
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7657444Z   - "hooks"
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7657698Z   - "canvases"  <-- but the `_prim_name` is "canvas"
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7658008Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7658706Z   So the `counter_key` on the canvas `PrimitiveMapping` must be `"canvases"` not `"canvas"` to match the result dict. Let me verify this by checking how the `counter_key` is set for each primitive mapping.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7659353Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7659712Z [336] ✓ grep(-C: 5, -n: true, output_mode: content, paths: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli, ...)

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7661736Z [338] ◐ Now I have a good understanding of the PR. Let me also look at what the canvas_integrator.py looks like and check the quickstart and why-apm docs to understand the narrative position.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7662447Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7662727Z   Let me now analyze from a growth hacker lens what I'm seeing:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7663072Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7663562Z   1. **New primitive added (canvas)**: This PR adds the canvas primitive for Copilot CLI extensions. It's experimental and Copilot-only.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7664118Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7664756Z   2. **Hook / Story angle**: The doc says "Canvases are typically produced by the `create-canvas` skill." This is the key hook - APM is now a distribution channel for Copilot CLI extensions. 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7665431Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7666764Z   The hook isn't being highlighted enough though. Looking at the documentation coverage, the new canvas integration page is well-structured with code examples, and the primitives matrix, changelog, and governance docs are all updated. But the README is missing any mention of canvas, which feels like a significant gap given this is a new primitive. The hero section doesn't even hint at this capability.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7668123Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7669634Z   The quickstart guide also doesn't cover it, and why-apm.md could do more to position canvas extensions as a Copilot-specific advantage. There's no template scaffolding either, so someone creating their first canvas extension has no reference implementation to work from. The docs mention that `create-canvas` produces these extensions, but there's no complete walkthrough showing the full flow from creation through packaging and installation.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7671312Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7673496Z   The `--trust-canvas-extensions` flag is a solid security measure, but the warning message about blocked canvases gets lost in the documentation rather than surfaced in the quickstart. There's also a missed opportunity here for a launch narrative—something like "you can now build a Copilot CLI extension, package it with APM, and have your whole team install it with a single command." Finally, there's a discoverability problem after installation: `apm list` doesn't show installed canvases yet, so users have no way to verify what they've actually installed.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7682527Z [344] ◐ Interesting - `apm pack` includes `--trust-canvas-extensions` as well, and importantly it does provide feedback to the user when canvases are blocked, distinguishing between two cases:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7683447Z   1. When the `canvas` feature flag IS enabled but `--trust-canvas-extensions` wasn't passed
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7683937Z   2. When the `canvas` feature flag is NOT enabled
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7684248Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7684788Z   So `apm pack` DOES give feedback. But `apm install` is silent in case 1 (flag on, dependency, no trust).
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7685257Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7685512Z   Wait, let me re-read the PR summary more carefully:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7686034Z   - "Flag on, dependency, no trust: blocked with diagnostic + hint to use `--trust-canvas-extensions`"
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7686489Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7686677Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7687285Z   So the diagnostic IS emitted from `_emit_trust_block`, but only when `diagnostics` is not None. If diagnostics is None in certain code paths, the warning gets silently dropped.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7687950Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7689440Z   The real UX problem I'm seeing is that when `--trust-canvas-extensions` is passed without the `canvas` feature flag being enabled, the method returns early at line 164-165 without any warning to the user. This means the flag silently does nothing, which is a poor developer experience—though it does match the stated design of being a silent no-op when the feature is disabled.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7690888Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7693454Z   The recommendation here is that when a user explicitly passes the trust flag but the feature gate is off, we should emit a contextual hint suggesting they enable the canvas experimental feature first. Additionally, `--trust-canvas-extensions` is missing from the CLI reference documentation entirely, which compounds the discoverability issue. The naming inconsistency is worth noting—`--trust-transitive-mcp` doesn't have an `[experimental]` prefix even though MCP features might also be experimental, so the `[experimental]` marker on `--trust-canvas-extensions` seems out of place. I should also verify whether `apm unpack` includes this flag as well.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7760355Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7763115Z   So there are some UX issues here. The `install.md` CLI reference is missing the new `--trust-canvas-extensions` flag from its options table, and the experimental features documentation doesn't list the canvas flag either. When `--trust-canvas-extensions` is passed explicitly but the `canvas` feature is disabled, the flag gets silently accepted without any feedback to the user. This is a UX gap—unlike `apm unpack` which provides diagnostics distinguishing between feature-off and feature-on-but-untrusted states, `apm install` should warn the user that they need to enable the `canvas` experimental feature first when they're actively trying to set a trust gate.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7765433Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7766249Z   I'm also noticing a naming inconsistency: `--trust-canvas-extensions` uses "canvas-extensions" while similar flags like `--trust-transitive-mcp` use just the concept name, though the help text references the "canvas" feature itself.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7767118Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7768355Z   The `[experimental]` prefix on the help text is another inconsistency—`--trust-transitive-mcp` doesn't have it, even though both are trust flags. The prefix arguably makes sense since the whole canvas feature is experimental, but it does conflate the experimental feature toggle with the trust flag itself.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7781434Z [392] ✓ $ grep -rn "canvas\|trust_canvas\|trust_canvas_extensions" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/install/pipeline.py | head -20
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7781916Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7782334Z [393] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive understanding of this PR. Let me analyze the architecture and compile my findings.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7782843Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7783068Z   **Summary of what this PR does:**
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7783340Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7783816Z   1. **New class**: `CanvasIntegrator(BaseIntegrator)` - Deploys Copilot canvas extension bundles into `.github/extensions/`
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7784400Z   2. **Trust-gate model**: Two independent gates:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7784764Z      - Feature flag: `apm experimental enable canvas` 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7785349Z      - Dependency trust opt-in: `--trust-canvas-extensions`
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7785875Z      - First-party canvases (from root project's `.apm/extensions/`) deploy freely once flag is on
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7786326Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7786512Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7787482Z   3. **Integration points**: The canvas integrator gets registered in the dispatch system, wired into the Copilot target's primitive mappings, added as an optional field to the bundle service, and threaded through the template pipeline to filter canvases during local bundle integration.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7788488Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7789430Z   4. **Unpacker and content detection**: Canvas filtering happens at unpack time, and the local content module now recognizes the `extensions/` directory as a primitive source, with `integrate_local_content` explicitly passing a canvas integrator instance marked as first-party.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7790414Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7791607Z   5. **Design validation**: The subclassing approach is clean and inherits all the base collision detection and file normalization logic correctly, and the trust gate placement in the target-specific integration function is the right spot to enforce first-party checks.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7792582Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7793261Z   6. **Consistency across boundaries**: Both the unpacker and local bundle handler independently block canvas paths to prevent any smuggling attempts, which is intentional and well-documented.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7793997Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7794608Z   7. **Optional canvas field**: The `canvas` parameter in `IntegratorBundle` is nullable to maintain compatibility with the ~16 existing construction sites that don't use it.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7795274Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7796568Z   The experimental flag gets re-read on each call to `integrate_canvases_for_target()`, which is intentional—it hits the config cache with just a couple of dict lookups after the initial load, so there's no performance concern. The `canvas` field being optional creates a sli... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 5883 chars]
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7797600Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7798043Z [394] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/in..., view_range: [370, 500])
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7798381Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7798615Z [395] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/in...)
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7798869Z

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7808878Z [407] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive picture of the auth surface touched by PR #1689. Let me analyze my findings:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7809347Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7809560Z   ### Auth/Trust Surface Assessment
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7809825Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7811652Z   1. **`trust_canvas` propagation**: The flag flows `CLI flag → InstallRequest → InstallContext (pipeline.py:511) → integrate_package_primitives (via `ctx.trust_canvas`) → CanvasIntegrator.integrate_canvases_for_target`. The propagation is correct and complete. The flag is never read from environment variables, never derived from user-controlled input at the integration level, and always comes from the operator's CLI invocation.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7813134Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7813907Z   2. **`is_first_party` decision - the key security invariant**: The PR's design note says "first-party status is decided by the call path, never inferred from a package-name string a dependency could spoof." Let me verify this:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7814929Z      - The only place `is_first_party=True` gets set is in `services.py:589` when integrating local content
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7815793Z      - Dependencies always default to `is_first_party=False` with an explicit comment in `services.py:357-361` stating that every dependency call requires the trust flag
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7816928Z      - Root primitives use the hardcoded `is_first_party=True` path through `_integrate_root_primitives`, while dependency packages go through a different code path that doesn't set this flag
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7817656Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7817972Z   , so the invariant holds and dependencies can't spoof first-party status
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7818838Z      - The `CanvasIntegrator` is purely local with no network operations, just copying files from the apm_modules directory to the extensions folder
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7819744Z      - The pipeline.py change only adds the `trust_canvas` field to `InstallContext` without affecting auth resolution or token lookup
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7820843Z      - The canvas deploy path writes only local file copies with no environment variables, tokens, or network requests exposed
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7821938Z      - Path safety is enforced through validation of canvas names, containment checks on both the target directory and individual files, and symlink rejection throughout the bundle discovery and file planning logic
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7822734Z

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7860276Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7861135Z   Actually, I think I have enough information to write the review. Let me now compile my findings and generate the JSON output.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7861789Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7862014Z   **Summary of architectural analysis:**
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7862292Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7862478Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7863911Z   The `CanvasIntegrator` cleanly extends `BaseIntegrator` by inheriting all the shared utilities like collision checking, file normalization, and cleanup logic, while layering on canvas-specific discovery, validation, and trust-gate routing. It maintains the same integration return type and follows the established patterns without introducing unnecessary complexity.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7865234Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7867070Z   The trust gate works through call-path routing rather than data spoofing—`is_first_party` is set by which code path invokes the integration, with local content setting it to `True` and all dependency paths defaulting to `False`. Canvas gets added to the dispatch table via a `PrimitiveDispatch` configuration that correctly routes internally by target name, and the `IntegratorBundle` preserves backward compatibility with a `None`-guard for the canvas field.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7868686Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7870808Z   The deployment cycle is atomic—it validates the bundle name, checks for path traversal and file collisions, then either adopts byte-identical files or copies everything, preventing partial writes. The experimental flag is read at function scope rather than module level, matching the required convention, while uninstall always runs using the managed files set to determine what to delete. The bundle path validation applies consistently across both integration and unpacking operations.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7872510Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7873081Z   There are a couple of rough edges though. When dependencies are blocked and `_emit_trust_block` fires, the skip... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2495 chars]

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7876675Z   Key findings from a growth lens:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7876935Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7878624Z   1. **No working canvas template in `templates/`**: The templates directory has `hello-world` and policy starters but nothing for canvas. The doc says canvases "are typically produced by the `create-canvas` skill" - but for the APM packaging side, there's no `templates/canvas-starter/` showing a minimal `extension.mjs` + `apm.yml` configuration. The docs show a directory tree but no file contents. This violates the anti-pattern "Examples that require the reader to fill in their own values without a working default."
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7880334Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7880514Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7881555Z   2. **CHANGELOG doesn't surface the session-reload requirement**: When users update APM, they check the CHANGELOG first. The entry skips the session-reload gotcha that's a known pain point ("I installed but my canvas isn't showing up!"), so it won't be discoverable there.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7883126Z   3. **canvas.md lacks the complete producer journey**: The doc fragments the story instead of showing the full flow from `create-canvas` through packaging and distribution to a team. It shows pieces but not how they connect end-to-end.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7884798Z   4. **No narrative framing for this launch**: This is APM's first primitive that extends the Copilot CLI itself—a significant positioning moment. The CHANGELOG reads like an engineer's note rather than something users would get excited about sharing.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7886477Z   5. **`apm list` visibility gap**: After install, there's no confirmation surface since canvases don't show up in `apm list` or `apm compile`. The PR might add install output, but if not, this becomes a silent deployment with no introspection path.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7895815Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7896208Z [437] ◐ The python-architect is still running after 180 seconds. Let me check the other agents while waiting.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7896603Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7896858Z [438] ✓ list_agents
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7896993Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7897140Z [439] ◐ Now I can see the key contrast:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7897408Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7898072Z   **`apm unpack`** correctly handles the case where canvases are blocked: it distinguishes between "feature off" and "trust not given", and provides a clear diagnostic with the next action.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7898792Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7899842Z   **`apm install`** does NOT have this behavior when `--trust-canvas-extensions` is explicitly passed but the `canvas` feature flag is off. The `integrate_canvases_for_target` method at line 164 of canvas_integrator.py silently returns empty when `is_enabled("canvas")` is false - even if `trust_canvas=True` was passed.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7901063Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7901373Z   But wait - there's a subtlety. For `apm install`, the behavior spec says:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7901742Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7901923Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7902205Z   - "Flag off: completely silent no-op (no warning, no skip count)"
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7902559Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7903445Z   The `canvas_blocked` count in `unpack` is derived differently - the unpack code always counts blocked canvas files whenever the feature is off or trust isn't given, but then the warning is only displayed if certain conditions are met in the unpack command handler.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7904374Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7906046Z   For `apm install` from a dependency, the behavior diverges: when the feature flag is off, it's a silent no-op with no feedback, but when the flag is on and trust is off, a diagnostic gets emitted. The real issue is that users can explicitly pass `--trust-canvas-extensions` to `apm install` but get zero feedback when the canvas feature is disabled - the flag is accepted but has no visible effect. The `apm unpack` command actually handles this better because it always evaluates the canvas logic regardless of the feature state.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7907729Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7909372Z   Looking at severity, this isn't a correctness regression or security issue - it's an intentional design choice per the PR description. So it warrants a recommended severity level. There's also a minor inconsistency with the `[experimental]` prefix on the help text for `--trust-canvas-extensions` - it's not applied to `--trust-transitive-mcp`, and it could mislead users into thinking the flag itself is experimental rather than the underlying feature.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7910994Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7911413Z   I'm also checking whether the full install path warns when can... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2802 chars]
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7911816Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7912295Z [440] ✓ $ grep -n "trust-canvas\|canvas" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/docs/src/content/docs/reference/experimental.md 2>/dev/null
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7912745Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7913998Z [441] ◐ Good - neither `drift.py` nor `template.py` pass `is_first_party` to `integrate_package_primitives`, so they both default to `False`. The `is_first_party=True` is only ever set by `integrate_local_content` in services.py, which is only called from `_integrate_root_primitives` in integrate.py, which is for the root project's own `.apm/` directory.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7915171Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7915367Z   This means:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7915700Z   1. Only the root project's own `.apm/` directory gets first-party treatment
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7916257Z   2. All dependency packages (including local-path deps) default to `is_first_party=False`
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7916684Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7916872Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7917570Z   3. The trust gate is correctly enforced - the offline bundle path in `integrate_local_bundle` has its own explicit check for both the experimental flag and trust flag, so there's no bypass there either.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7918320Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7921628Z   Now I'm ready to finalize my findings. The auth and trust infrastructure is solid with no blocking issues, though there's a recommended clarification: the comments saying "first-party (root/local) canvases" could be misleading since only the root project's `.apm/` directory actually gets first-party treatment, while local-path dependencies still go through the trust gate. So "local" in the `is_first_party` context actually refers to the root project's `_local` package, not local-path dependencies—the comment's use of "root/local" is colloquial rather than technical, which is a bit confusing but not a security problem. I'm settling on a JSON response that marks this as active with a summary noting the call-path control is correct and the comment ambiguity is worth clarifying, plus one recommended finding about that imprecision.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7924491Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7926097Z   Let me also verify the experimental flag can't be bypassed—the `is_enabled("canvas")` check reads from APM config, so a dependency could theoretically inject into the config to enable it, but that's a pre-existing risk not introduced here. And for the file copying in `_deploy_bundle`, the source files come from the already-downloaded bundle in `apm_modules/`, so there's no remote fetch happening at that stage.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.7987049Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.7988429Z   Actually, looking at this more carefully, there's a layered defense here: `validate_path_segments` already rejects `.` and `..`, the regex only allows alphanumeric characters plus dots, hyphens, and underscores, there's a check against hidden names starting with a dot, and then `ensure_path_within` provides an additional guard. So this is redundant but not necessarily wrong.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8072126Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8073388Z   Actually, I realize there IS a gap: `canvases` counter in `deltas` (populated by `template.py`) is never accumulated by the integrate phase. This means the finalize phase's `ctx.installed_count += 1` if `any(int_result[k] > 0 for k in mutation_keys)` (from template.py's `if any(int_result[k] > 0 for k in mutation_keys): deltas["installed"] = 1`) does correctly track that a package was "installed" if it had any canvases. 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8073480Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8074325Z   So `installed_count` is tracking correctly, but there's no aggregated `total_canvases_integrated` metric being rolled up in the finalize phase's verbose logging. Let me check what the finalize phase is actually doing.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8077522Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8077731Z [514] ◐ Now I understand the hash recording for canvas files:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8077814Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8078812Z   1. **Local bundle path (`integrate_local_bundle`)**: Canvas files are filtered by `is_canvas_bundle_path` before the copy loop. If they pass the trust gate, they ARE copied via `shutil.copy2` (line 931) and hashed at line 943: `deployed_hashes[record] = compute_file_hash(dest)`. So canvas files in local bundles DO get their hashes recorded.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8078900Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8079852Z   2. **Normal install pipeline (`integrate_package_primitives`)**: Canvas files are deployed via `CanvasIntegrator.integrate_canvases_for_target` which uses `shutil.copyfile`. The `target_paths` from the result are added to `deployed` at line 371: `deployed.append(_deployed_path_entry(tp, project_root, targets))`. 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8079938Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8080693Z   But I'm only getting the path here, not the hash. I need to trace through the normal pipeline to see where `compute_deployed_hashes` gets called for canvas files, or if there's another hash computation step I'm missing.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8080697Z

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8081350Z

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8091781Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8092136Z [535] ◐ Now I have a complete picture of the logging architecture. Let me do a comprehensive analysis:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8092221Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8092320Z   **Summary of findings:**
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8092413Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8092673Z   1. **`_emit_trust_block` uses `CATEGORY_WARNING` instead of `CATEGORY_POLICY` (recommended)**
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8092886Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8093777Z   The trust block in `canvas_integrator.py` (line 416) calls `diagnostics.warn()` which puts the diagnostic in `CATEGORY_WARNING`. The `_render_warning_group` in `diagnostics.py` (line 425-430) renders each item with its full message inline using `_rich_warning(f"  [!] {pkg_prefix}{d.message}")`.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8093863Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8093945Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8095489Z   The trust block message itself is already quite informative and includes the fix, but there's a semantic mismatch here. Trust and executable blocks should really live in `CATEGORY_POLICY` since that's what the MCP trust-blocking uses, and canvas is essentially the same concept. The `_render_policy_group` renderer would display this as `[x] N dependency blocked by org policy` in red and bold, which feels more appropriate for blocked executable code. That said, this is more of a design choice than an actual bug.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8095581Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8096819Z   The author might have deliberately chosen a yellow warning instead of a red policy block because this isn't security blocking in the policy sense—it's a trust opt-in for a feature, which is a reasonable distinction. The trust block message itself is quite long when rendered inline, and it gets cut off awkwardly when displayed.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8096904Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8097907Z   There's also a duplication issue: the package name appears twice in the output because `_emit_trust_block` includes the package name in the message body, but `_render_warning_group` adds it again as a prefix via `pkg_prefix`. So the rendered output shows `[!] [my-pkg] Blocked ... from 'my-pkg':` with the package name appearing redundantly.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8097990Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8099020Z   I'm also noticing that while `canvas_blocked` in `UnpackResult` is properly surfaced with distinct messages for when the flag is on versus off, there's a terminology inconsistency between the two warning messages: one says "not unpacked by default" while the other says "not deployed from bundle... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 9119 chars]
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8099031Z

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8117686Z [543] ◐ So for the normal install pipeline, `compute_deployed_hashes` is called at line 142 which hashes ALL deployed files including canvas files. Canvas files are tracked in `ctx.package_deployed_files` via the `deployed.append(...)` call in `integrate_package_primitives`. So canvas files DO get their hashes recorded in `lockfile.dependencies[dep_key].deployed_file_hashes`.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8117781Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8118912Z   This means my earlier concern about canvas files not getting hashes was wrong for the normal install pipeline. Canvas files deployed via `integrate_package_primitives` → `CanvasIntegrator` → tracked in `target_paths` → `deployed` → `ctx.package_deployed_files` → `compute_deployed_hashes` = hashes recorded.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8131491Z   2. **`/clear` session reload claim may mislead users** (recommended): The Copilot `/clear` slash command clears conversation context but doesn't restart the extension discovery mechanism. If Copilot only discovers extensions at session start, running `/clear` won't pick up new canvases—users need to start a fresh Copilot CLI session instead. The source code comment mentions discovery "in the session" but doesn't clarify whether `/clear` triggers it.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8142222Z      - Searching the test files for "canvas" in both the unit and integration drift tests returns no matches.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8143056Z      - `_canvas_deploy_prefixes` is completely untested, creating a silent-drift risk where the `apm audit --check drift` command could skip canvas paths without any verification, and if this code breaks, deployed canvases would be incorrectly flagged as orphaned or modified.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8162358Z 
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8162840Z [571] ◐ No integration tests for canvas. All canvas tests are unit tests. This is a significant gap for a security-critical surface.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8162924Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8163259Z   The canvas trust gate sits at the install-pipeline surface (not a simple function), and the only tests for it are:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8163523Z   - `tests/unit/integration/test_canvas_integrator.py` - unit tests for `CanvasIntegrator`
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8163740Z   - `tests/unit/test_unpacker.py` - unit tests for `unpack_bundle` canvas gate
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8163994Z   - `tests/unit/install/test_install_local_bundle.py` - unit tests for local bundle handler
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8164078Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8164262Z   There are NO integration-with-fixtures or e2e tests for canvas:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8164340Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8164422Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8164664Z   - No test that runs the full `apm install` CLI and verifies a dependency canvas is blocked
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8164921Z   - No test that runs `apm install` with `--trust-canvas-extensions` and verifies canvas deploys
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8165086Z   - No test that runs `apm unpack` and verifies canvas blocking
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8165169Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8166012Z   For a feature that deploys arbitrary executable Node.js code—the highest-risk primitive in APM—the lack of integration tests means the trust gate could be bypassed by a change in the pipeline wiring without any test catching it.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8166099Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8166422Z   Looking at my final assessment, the core trust gate logic itself is sound, so there are no blocking findings here.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8166506Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8168263Z   On the recommended side, I'm noting that there are no integration-with-fixtures tests for the canvas trust gate. Since this is the primary security primitive blocking arbitrary executable Node.js code from dependencies, all the gate tests being unit-level with mocks and temp directories leaves a gap. A regression in the pipeline wiring—like forgetting to pass `is_first_party` or `trust_canvas` through a new code path—could silently bypass the gate without any test catching it.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8168480Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8169746Z   I'm also flagging a potential false negative in `is_canvas_bundle_path` where crafted bundle lockfiles could potentially place canvas... `shutil.copyfile` does support a `follow_symlinks` parameter that defaults to True, but setting it to False copies the symlink itself rather than its target, which doesn't solve the TOCTOU vulnerability. The real fix woul... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3834 chars]

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8171968Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8172136Z   Let me now compose the mermaid class diagram and flowchart.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8172219Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8172311Z   **Class Diagram:**
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8172389Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8172804Z   Problem space: BaseIntegrator hierarchy + PrimitiveDispatch + TargetProfile/PrimitiveMapping + IntegratorBundle + InstallContext
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8172888Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8172983Z   Key relationships:
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8173191Z   - `CanvasIntegrator` extends `BaseIntegrator` (added by this PR, touched)
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8173356Z   - `BaseIntegrator` uses `IntegrationResult` (returns it)
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8173651Z   - `PrimitiveDispatch` references `integrator_class: type[BaseIntegrator]` (dispatch table entry)
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8173735Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8173816Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8174298Z   - `TargetProfile` holds a mapping of primitives including the new canvas entry, while `IntegratorBundle` now optionally contains a canvas integrator instance
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8174639Z   - The dispatch loop in `integrate_package_primitives` ties these together, pulling from both the bundle and profile
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8174726Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8175321Z   The PR touches CanvasIntegrator (new class), IntegratorBundle (adds canvas field), the canvas primitive mapping, and the integrators registry, while leaving the other integrator types untouched.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8175404Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8177031Z   The install flow starts at `apm install`, moves through the targets phase where the CanvasIntegrator gets instantiated, then into the integrate phase where `integrate_package_primitives` dispatches to `integrate_canvases_for_target`, which applies a series of gates: experimental flag check, target validation (copilot only), scope filtering (non-USER), trust validation (first-party or trusted canvas), and finally deployment with name validation, path checks, file planning, collision detection, and content comparison.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8177119Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8178731Z   For local content, the flow bypasses the trust gate entirely by routing through `_integrate_root_project` → `integrate_local_content` with `is_first_party=True`, while bundle paths go through unpacking and canvas bundle path filtering. The codebase uses base/subclass inheritance for the integrator, dataclasses as value objects for results and mappings, and a chain-of-responsibility pattern for the four-gate canvas trust validation.

2026-06-07T11:20:27.8209160Z       class CanvasIntegrator {
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8209253Z           <<ConcreteTemplate>>
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8209339Z           ...
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8209424Z       }
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8209646Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8209727Z       ...
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8209812Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8209928Z       class CanvasIntegrator:::touched
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8210039Z       class IntegratorBundle:::touched
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8210173Z       classDef touched fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d47600
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8210256Z   ```
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8210338Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8211528Z   The `class CanvasIntegrator:::touched` line is a standalone class style declaration after the class definition block, which is valid in GitHub's mermaid renderer. When you define `class CanvasIntegrator { ... }` and then later write `class CanvasIntegrator:::touched`, mermaid applies the style to the existing class rather than creating a duplicate.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8211617Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8212468Z   The key distinction is that `:::` on relationship lines like `A *-- B:::touched` causes issues with GitHub's parser, but standalone style declarations like `class Name:::touched` work fine. The correct approach is to define the class body first, then apply the style separately afterward.
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8212558Z   
2026-06-07T11:20:27.8214778Z   Now I need to write out the complete JSON response, being careful to properly escape newlines and quotes since the mermaid diagrams will be embedded as strings. I also need to make sure the diagrams avoid Unicode characters, styling on relationship lines, and other invalid syntax patterns. Counting characters in the summary to verify it stays under 200... Writing class structure... I see the current rewritten thinking contains encoded characters that appear corrupted. The next thinking also contains encoded characters followed by a note about trimming, then a fragment about CanvasIntegrator fitting a BaseIntegrator subclass... 2026-06-07T11:20:27.8270247Z     "arbitration": "All eight panelists converge cleanly: no blocking findings, no correctness regression, no architectural fault that compounds. Python-architect confirms the `BaseIntegrator` subclass pattern is followed; the DI inconsistency in `integrate_local_content` is a testability concern only, not a runtime defect. The overall shape of this PR is solid enough to land after the draft phase with targeted pre-exit fixes.\n\nTwo gaps stand above the rest on risk grounds. First, `security.md` now carries an actively false claim: 'APM does not execute scripts.' Canvas is exactly that -- executable Node.js deployed by APM. Doc-writer identified a double failure: the cross-link from `canvas.md` delivers no canvas content, and the statement it implicitly endorses is incorrect for the new surface. An enterprise auditor landing on that cross-link gets a false assurance picture. This is a documentation trust defect that must be corrected before the draft exits -- removing the dangling cross-link and adding a canvas exception callout to `security.md` under 'What APM does NOT do.' Second, the integration tier of the trust gate has zero test coverage. Supply-chain and test-coverage converge on this: for a surface that deploys executable code, APM's tier-floor matrix requires `integration-with-fixtures`. All existing coverage is unit-only (monkeypatched config, `tmp_path`). A future refactor mis-threading `trust_canvas` or `is_first_party` would not be caught until runtime. Th... to specify what pull request data needs to be retrieved from GitHub. \nPossible options: \n 1. get - Get details of a specific pull request.\n 2. get_diff - Get the diff of a pull request.\n 3. get_status - Get combined commit status of a head commit in a pull request.\n 4. get_files - Get the list of files changed in a pull request. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.\n 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. Returns threads with metadata (isResolved, isOutdated, isCollapsed) and their associated comments. Use cursor-based pagination (perPage, after) to control results.\n 6. get_reviews - Get the reviews on a pull request. When asked for review comments, use get_review_comments method.\n 7. get_comments - Get comments on a pull request. Use this if user doesn't specifically want review comments. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.\n 8. get_check_runs - Get check runs for the head commit of a pull request. 2026-06-07T11:20:28.0596939Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-07T10:53:01.601Z","event":"rpc_response","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"IN","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":1689,\"title\":\"feat(install): experimental Copilot canvas extensions\",\"body\":\"# feat(install): experimental Copilot canvas extensions\\n\\n## TL;DR\\n\\nA GitHub Copilot CLI **canvas** (a directory with an executable `extension.mjs`, produced by the `create-canvas` skill) had no way to ship through APM — there was no primitive, no integrator, and no target mapping carrying it from a package into a consumer\\u0026#39;s `.github/extensions/`. This PR adds an experimental, Copilot-only `canvas` primitive so a package can declare `.apm/extensions//` and have `apm install` / `apm pack` deploy it verbatim to `.github/extensions//`, where Copilot CLI discovers it in-session. The surface is protected by two independent gates — an experimental feature flag and a dependency-trust opt-in for executable code — and is fail-closed on every path that materialises canvas files.\\n\\n\\u0026gt; [!NOTE]\\n\\u0026gt; Experimental and opt-in twice over: nothing deploys unless the operator runs `apm experimental enable canvas`, and a **dependency\\u0026#39;s** canvas additionally requires `--trust-canvas-extensions`. No existing install behaviour changes when the flag is off.\\n\\n## Problem (WHY)\\n\\n- [x] The `create-canvas` skill emits a Copilot CLI extension, but APM has no canvas/extension primitive — instructions, prompts, agents, skills, hooks, commands, plugins, and MCP are the only known types. There is nowhere for a canvas to live or be routed.\\n- [x] Copilot CLI only discovers extensions in immediate subdirectories of `.github/extensions//`. Without an integrator that targets that path, a packaged canvas is never available in the session.\\n- [!] A canvas `extension.mjs` is arbitrary executable Node code. Shipping one transitively via a dependency is a code-execution surface that must not deploy silently.\\n\\nWhy these matter: an experimental flag alone is a *feature-availability* switch, not a security control — the experimental subsystem states flags [\\u0026#34;MUST NOT gate security-critical behaviour\\u0026#34;](https://github.com/microsoft/apm/blob/main/src/apm_cli/core/experimental.py#L17-L22). Executable-code trust therefore needs its own gate, mirroring the existing `--trust-transitive-mcp` boundary.\\n\\n## Approach (WHAT)\\n\\n| # | Change |\\n|---|--------|\\n| 1 | New `canvas` experimental flag (feature availability; default off). |\\n| 2 | New `--trust-canvas-extensions` install flag plumbed end-to-end (request → context → integrate phase), mirroring `trust_transitive_mcp`. |\\n| 3 | New `CanvasIntegrator` (Copilot-only, project-scope) with atomic per-bundle deploy, strict name validation, and containment checks. |\\n| 4 | Target mapping + dispatch entry so `canvas` routes only on the `copilot` target; user scope and non-Copilot targets are hard-unsupported. |\\n| 5 | Fail-closed enforcement of **both** gates on the offline `apm install ` and `apm unpack` paths, so a vendored bundle cannot smuggle executable canvas code. |\\n| 6 | First-party status decided by the call path, never inferred from a package-name string a dependency could spoof. |\\n\\n## Implementation (HOW)\\n\\n- **`src/apm_cli/integration/canvas_integrator.py`** (new) — `CanvasIntegrator` scans `.apm/extensions/*/extension.mjs`, validates each name, applies the trust gate for dependency sources, and deploys each bundle atomically (plan → directory-collision guard → unmanaged-collision skip → adopt-if-byte-identical → copy). `sync_for_target` is deliberately **not** flag-gated so uninstall always removes a previously-deployed canvas. Also exports `is_canvas_bundle_path` shared with the offline paths.\\n- **`src/apm_cli/install/services.py`** — dispatch threads `trust_canvas` and an explicit `is_first_party` into the canvas call; `integrate_local_content` passes `is_first_party=True`; dependency/template/drift callers default to `False`. `integrate_local_bundle` enforces both gates and routes `extensions/` only to Copilot.\\n- **`src/apm_cli/bundle/unpacker.py`** — `unpack_bundle` gains `trust_canvas` and a `canvas_blocked` count; drops canvas paths unless both gates hold (fail closed).\\n- **`src/apm_cli/bundle/plugin_exporter.py`** — collects `extensions/` into the pack so `apm pack` round-trips a canvas (files stay inert until a consumer gates them).\\n- **`core/experimental.py`, `commands/install.py`, `commands/pack.py`, `integration/targets.py`, `integration/dispatch.py`, `install/{context
2026-06-07T11:20:28.0612178Z ...
